content: As the Chief of Operations and the ultimate commander of the Special Task Force (STF), AIG Shah's claim that his role was limited because the IGP was giving direct orders to the field is viewed as unprofessional. He failed to advise the IGP against breaking the chain of command. Furthermore, as the head of the department responsible for the STF, he failed to ensure they had clear Rules of Engagement (ROE) and scenario-based training. This lack of guidance contributed to the four-hour-long indiscriminate firing. However, the Commission recommends he be rewarded for his leadership in successfully defending the Police Headquarters at Naxal from attackers on September 9.
APF AIG Narayan Datta Paudel

AIG Paudel, as the Operation Chief of the Armed Police Force, claimed his forces only fired 19 warning shots in the air. This is factually inconsistent with autopsy reports showing numerous protesters killed by gunshot wounds. He failed to play a role in 'Damage Control' during the peak of the violence and did not issue any 'Cease Fire' instructions. Like his counterparts, his failure to implement ROE and ensure coordination with Nepal Police on September 9 led to significant state losses.
DIG Om Bahadur Rana and DIG Bishwa Adhikari

DIG Rana (then Acting Chief of Ranipokhari) was heard by the CDO ordering units to 'open fire to take control as necessary' after the curfew. He made no effort to monitor the casualties or stop the lethal force during the four-hour conflict.

DIG Bishwa Adhikari (then SSP of Kathmandu District) is held responsible for the weak initial deployment at the Everest Hotel barricade. His strategy of 'low visibility' backfired when the crowd outnumbered the police. He failed to clear the tall buildings in the Baneshwor area for security and admitted to not implementing a Layered Defense system. His claim that he only learned of the deaths at 2:30 PM reveals a massive failure in his communication and intelligence channels.
Armed Police Force (APF) Deputy Inspector General Suresh Kumar Shrestha

As the Valley Chief of the APF, DIG Shrestha failed to play a significant role in damage control during the four-hour clash on September 8 (Bhadra 23). A more serious failure occurred on September 9, when weapons and ammunition were looted from the Min Bhawan Security Base. The Commission questions why highly sensitive materials were not secured or, if evacuation was impossible, neutralized/destroyed to prevent them from falling into criminal hands. Furthermore, DIG Shrestha failed to conduct an internal investigation into the command failures that led to this loss of state weaponry.

The Commission emphasizes that for an armed organization, having clear Rules of Engagement (ROE) and scenario-based training is essential. Without an ROE, field commanders face ambiguity, leading to disproportionate force, breakdown in the chain of command, and human rights violations. The lack of initiative to implement such standards led to the indiscriminate use of force on September 8.
National Investigation Department (NID) Officials: Krishna Prasad Khanal and Riben Kumar Gachhedar

Both the Valley Division Chief (Khanal) and the Kathmandu District Chief (Gachhedar) failed in their fundamental duty of intelligence gathering. They were unaware of how Gen Z protesters used digital platforms to coordinate. Despite their claims of being 'diligent,' their failure to provide accurate situational assessments meant other security agencies could not formulate effective strategies, leading to high casualties.
SSP Deep Shamsher J.B.R. (Nepal Police)

SSP J.B.R. served as the commander inside the Federal Parliament premises. His claim that police only used non-lethal weapons and fired in self-defense is contradicted by autopsy reports and victim testimony. He failed to implement a layered defense system or coordinate the 400+ personnel under his command. His inability to order a 'Cease Fire' during the four-hour shooting outside the gates demonstrates a lack of professional leadership.
SP Rishiram Kandel (STF) and SP Jivan K.C. (APF)

SP Kandel (Special Task Force) denied using live fire, a claim refuted by hospital records. He also failed to ensure that STF communication sets were compatible with local police units. SP Jivan K.C., the overall APF commander for Kathmandu, failed to coordinate with Nepal Police. His claim that his forces only conducted 'warning shots' is logically inconsistent with the high number of gunshot casualties found in the field.
Analysis of Liability and Recommendations for Prosecution

The Commission finds that the 'indiscriminate force' used resulted in irreparable human loss. To prevent future impunity, those responsiblefrom the Prime Minister to field commandersmust face legal consequences. Under Article 16 of the Constitution, the state has a duty to protect life; instead, state actions on September 8 and 9 led to its destruction.

Despite the shooting lasting four hours and hospitals being overwhelmed, no senior officialincluding the PM, Home Minister, or Home Secretaryissued orders to stop the fire. This indifference by the 'guardians of the people' is characterized as Criminal Negligence. Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak’s statement that he 'discussed' the matter with the PM and Army Chief is insufficient, as no concrete steps were taken to restore peace.

Regarding the defense of 'necessity' (protecting state buildings or police lives), the Commission notes that almost all victims were shot outside the boundary walls on public sidewalks. No protesters who breached the initial line were shot; force was applied to those already outside or even watching from nearby buildings (e.g., the shop owner in Sunari and a tailor in Baneshwor). The 'self-defense' argument is therefore invalid.

The Commission also highlights a massive failure on September 9. By lifting the social media ban on the night of the 8th without a security assessment, Communication Minister Prithvi Subba Gurung and Chief Secretary Eknarayan Aryal allowed violence to spread nationwide. The government's 'haphazard' decision-making turned a local protest into a national disaster involving arson and looting.

Finally, the Commission notes that the Prime Minister's attempt to shift all blame onto 'local administration' is unacceptable. The policy of suppression and the subsequent negligence in managing the aftermath directly caused the national catastrophe.
National Legal Provisions and Arrangements

Section 6 of the National Penal Code, 2017 (2074 BS) establishes a general principle of criminal justice: an act performed in accordance with the law or deemed excusable by law shall not be considered an offense. A critical question arises whether security personnel and government officials involved in the September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082) incident can claim full immunity under this provision.

Section 24 provides for the Right to Private Defense, granting every individual the right to protect their own or another's life and property from unlawful harm. However, this right is contingent upon a 'reasonable belief' that immediate action is necessary to prevent such harm.

Conversely, Section 25 restricts this right, stating that the right to private defense is not available in various circumstances, specifically prohibiting the use of force exceeding what is 'reasonably necessary' under Section 25(3).

While Section 26(1) generally prohibits taking a life during private defense, Section 26(2)(f) provides an exception for security personnel. It states that if a security officer, acting under the orders of a competent authority to protect individuals or public/government property, must immediately retaliate against an attack, a resulting death shall not be considered a crime. This aligns with the Essential Goods Protection Act, 1952, which suggests a graduated responseaiming below the knee to incapacitate. Firing with the intent to kill is strictly a last resort.

In the case of the protester deaths outside the Parliament premises on September 8, the evidence fails to confirm that the government side acted within the lawful bounds of the Right to Private Defense as defined in Section 26(2).

The Commission notes that the right to life provided by Article 16 of the Constitution and the freedom of peaceful assembly under Article 17 were effectively suspended by the government's actions on September 8. Despite public allegations regarding the shooting of minors, the then-Prime Minister and Home Minister continue to deny issuing such orders.

Legally, the Local Administration Act, 1971 (2028 BS) grants only the Chief District Officer (CDO) the authority to order firing. However, the Prime Minister and Home Minister maintain direct control over the Ministry of Home Affairs and security agencies. Despite Home Minister Ramesh Lekhak reportedly informing the Prime Minister once the shooting began at 12:15 PM, no effort was made by the executive leadership to stop the four-hour-long gunfire, even as hospitals like Civil, Trauma Center, and Bir were overwhelmed with victims.

The investigation revealed that at 12:40 PM on September 8, then-IGP Chandra Kuber Khapung issued an order via the communication set to 'use necessary force' without waiting for formal curfew orders. Following this, the Kathmandu District Police Chief and Acting Chief of Valley Police DIG Om Bahadur Rana also ordered force. While the IGP claimed in his statement that he had no legal role in ordering force, his direct commands over the radio preceded the escalation to lethal fire.

On September 9, the situation worsened as police offices were attacked and weapons looted. The leadership's orders to 'remain safe' and use 'only tear gas'including instructions to release detaineesresulted in a breakdown of morale and state authority, leaving the country in a state of virtual lawlessness.

As the head of state and the ultimate recipient of intelligence via the National Investigation Department (NID), the Prime Minister is presumed to have been aware of the 17 deaths and thousands of injuries. The failure to intervene despite this knowledge points toward significant executive negligence.
International Human Rights Law Context

content.content: The incident violated Articles 19 (Freedom of Expression) and 20(1) (Right to Peaceful Assembly) of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), 1948.
It violated Articles 6 (Right to Life), 19 (Freedom of Expression), and 21 (Right to Peaceful Assembly) of the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR), 1966.

content: Furthermore, the state failed to uphold the Directive Principles under Article 51 of the Constitution of Nepal, particularly regarding the protection of human rights and the maintenance of the rule of law. According to UN Human Rights Committee General Comment No. 31, Nepal has failed to meet the following established obligations:
General Comment No. 31 [80]
This comment outlines the nature of the general legal obligations imposed on States Parties to the Covenant, adopted on March 29, 2004. Key requirements include:

content.content: Preventing violations committed by security forces.
Conducting prompt and independent investigations into killings.
Initiating prosecution or disciplinary action against those found guilty.
Providing effective remedies to the families of victims.

content: Since the head of the government at the time of the incident (the Prime Minister) resigned and left office, that specific administration was unable to conduct the investigation. The subsequent government began prosecutions for the September 9 (Bhadra 24) events, and this Inquiry Commission was immediately formed. It is critical to note that the legal obligations mentioned above apply even if the Prime Minister, Home Minister, or other high-ranking officials did not directly order the use of force or the shooting.

The investigation reveals that security forces directly violated these provisions. While the government provided free medical treatment and reached financial assistance agreements with victims' families and the 'Gen Z' protesters (including a 10-point agreement and the formation of a Gen Z Council), there are discrepancies in administrative records. Although it was claimed that the K.P. Oli cabinet decided on September 8 to form a judicial inquiry commission, no such decision was reflected in the official minutes of that day's meeting.

Public skepticism remains high regarding the government's investigation style. This Commission officially informed the government on October 7, 2025, that criminal investigations and prosecutions should not wait for the Commission's final report. However, the release of suspects on bail without proper prosecution has been viewed as a mockery of the rule of law and indicates gross government negligence.

When executive heads fail to prevent foreseeable harm, control subordinates, or ensure accountability, they violate their fundamental duty to protect citizens. Human Rights Watch commented on the September 8 incident, stating that Nepal police used 'excessive, unlawful, and lethal force,' resulting in at least 19 deaths and hundreds of injuries during protests against corruption and a social media ban.
Failures of the State During the September 8 Incident at Parliament Premises:

content.content: The majority of protesters were unarmed.
Lethal weapons and metal bullets were used indiscriminately.
Effective warnings were not given before the use of weapons, and non-lethal measures (water cannons, batons, barricades, warning shots, or dialogue) were not sufficiently or correctly applied.
There was a complete collapse of human rights accountability across the police, armed police, army, administrative, and executive leadership.
The Special Task Force (STF) of the Nepal Police failed to follow established 'Rules of Engagement' required for armed personnel.

content: Questions of Accountability for September 8 (Bhadra 23):

While the protest began peacefully, it escalated at the south gate of the Parliament. However, data from Civil Hospital shows the first gunshot victim arrived at 12:07 PM, and others followed by 12:15 PMbefore a curfew was even declared. By early afternoon, news of deaths and hundreds of injuries had already reached the media.

Despite this, no state organ (District Security Committee, Central Security Committee, or National Security Council) took steps during the day to stop the firing or prevent further human loss. There was an apparent lack of consultation between the CDO, Home Secretary, IGPs, NID Chief, Home Minister, and Prime Minister regarding damage control. This collective indifference makes both the political and administrative leadership fully responsible for the casualties on that day.

Responsible leaders like the Prime Minister and Home Minister cannot claim a lack of 'direct command' as a defense. Under constitutional and international human rights standards, leadership is held responsible because the outcomes were foreseeable and the government failed to control its armed forces.

Under the mandate provided to this Commission, it has been observed that the events of September 8, 2025 (Bhadra 23, 2082), involving the 'Gen Z' youth protest, resulted in human rights violations alongside the tragic loss of life. It is the core duty of this Commission to uncover the truth regarding these incidents and the subsequent human casualties.

Human rights are the essential rights required for a human being to live with dignity. These are considered inherent, birthright, and inalienable. They encompass civil, political, economic, social, and cultural dimensions. The Human Rights Commission Act, 1997 (2053 BS) defines these as 'rights relating to life, liberty, equality, and dignity of the individual guaranteed by the Constitution and other prevailing laws, as well as rights embodied in international treaties to which Nepal is a party.' Regardless of whether they are called natural, fundamental, or constitutional rights, their focal point remains human value and the right to life.
